meta.title: R-Class Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: R-Class Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes R-Class W251 — a spacious, seven-seat MPV that never quite found its market. Full coverage of OM642 V6 diesel reliability, M272 V6 petrol timing chain history, and the honest economics of replacing an engine on a car worth less than the repair. Backed by documented failure patterns and specialist knowledge [PATTERN DATA].

metrics.ourCall: 🔴 The R-Class rule: check the scrap value first — a £3,200–£5,500 reconditioned engine against a £1,500–£5,000 car means the honest answer is often "don't repair it."
OM642 V6 diesel — the well-understood Mercedes V6 with 880 UK-wide enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED]; proven and durable if the oil cooler seals have been addressed.
M272 V6 petrol — timing chain stretch on earlier examples (pre-2008) and intake manifold swirl flap issues; well-documented and costly to fix.
R320 CDI (2009–2013 facelift) with OM642 — the facelift addressed the OM642's oil cooler seal design, and diesel torque suits the R-Class's 2.2-tonne kerb weight perfectly.
OM642 oil cooler seal leak — the V6 diesel's defining weak point; 880 UK-wide enquiries across all Mercedes models in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].
⚠️ Niche, Check Value — the R-Class is the Mercedes no one remembers, and its ownership economics reflect that: a specialist car for specialist buyers who know exactly what they're getting into.

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The R-Class is a spacious, capable seven-seater that's now cheap to buy — but the engine replacement cost will frequently exceed the car's value. Buy it for the interior space, not the investment case, and budget for maintenance that might not be financially recoverable.

rows.value: OM642 (880 UK-wide enquiries [MB-VERIFIED]; R-Class share is pattern-data tier)
OM642 V6 diesel — proven and durable with documented maintenance
M272 V6 petrol — timing chain stretch on pre-2008 examples

generations.h2: Mercedes R-Class — One Generation, Multiple Powertrains
generations.subHeadline: The W251 R-Class ran from 2006 to 2013 with a single chassis but a split life: pre-facelift (2006–2009) and facelift (2010–2013). The engines remained largely the same — the difference is in the electrical architecture and the OM642's oil cooler seal design.

cards.title: W251 R-Class (2006–2013)
cards.meta: 2006–2013 • OM642 3.0 V6 diesel • M272 3.5 V6 petrol • M273 5.5 V8 petrol • M156 6.2 AMG V8
cards.verdict: The R-Class is a seven-seat, 2.2-tonne MPV that drives like an E-Class — spacious, comfortable, and practical. The OM642 diesel is the engine to buy: torquey, reliable, and with the facelift's improved oil cooler seal design, less prone to the V6's signature leak. The M272 petrol is smooth but thirstier and carries the timing-chain caveat on pre-2008 cars. The R63 AMG (M156 V8) is a 510hp curiosity — only 200 built, and maintenance costs are S-Class AMG levels. Buy a 2010–2013 facelift R320 CDI with full service history and budget £800–£1,500 for an oil cooler seal replacement if it hasn't been done.

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes R-Class Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to the Mercedes R-Class W251 — verified against manufacturer specifications, with specialist cost ranges and reliability guidance.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM642 rating: High confidence — based on 880 UK-wide enquiries across all Mercedes models in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], cross-referenced with Mercedes TSBs and specialist interviews. The oil cooler seal weakness is well-documented and well-understood. M272, M273, M156 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented failure patterns and specialist interviews [PATTERN DATA], not proprietary R-Class-specific volume data. Data note (internal): * Correction applied: R-Class model-level enquiry data is absent from EM_Proprietary_Data. All figures for OM642, M272, M273, M156 are UK-wide Mercedes figures, not R-Class-specific. This page uses [PATTERN DATA] for all R-Class-specific claims and [MB-VERIFIED] only when citing shared-engine UK-wide data. * Correction applied: The R-Class never received the OM654 diesel — it was discontinued in 2013, before the OM654 launched. Any mention of OM654 in an R-Class context is incorrect and should be removed. * No further corrections — source data matched live verification.

commonProblems.subHeadline: Real UK failure patterns from our 2025 enquiry data and documented specialist knowledge — what goes wrong, at what mileage, and what it costs to fix.

problems.description: The defining OM642 weakness across the R-Class range. An O-ring fails in the "V" of the engine, causing oil to pool under the intake manifold — labour-intensive to access but rarely catastrophic if caught early. 880 UK-wide enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED]. £800–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].
Pre-2008 R-Class M272 engines (R350, R280) are known for timing chain stretch — cold-start rattle is the early warning. The repair requires significant disassembly. £1,500–£2,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

The swirl flap motor on the OM642 intake manifold fails, triggering an engine management light and reduced power. The repair involves replacing the motor or, on earlier cars, removing the flaps entirely (a common specialist modification). £500–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].

problems.issue: M272 Intake Manifold Swirl Flap Failure
problems.description: The M272 petrol V6 has its own swirl flap weakness — the plastic linkage fails, causing the flaps to stick. Left unrepaired, it can cause a vacuum leak and rough running. £600–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].

The OM642's injectors can fail at higher mileages (120k–150k), causing rough idle, black smoke, and poor economy. Individual injector replacement is straightforward. £300–£800 per injector [MB-QUOTE].

On the V8 petrol engines (R500, R63 AMG), the camshaft adjusters can wear, causing a rattle on start-up and, in extreme cases, timing issues. A specialist-only repair. £1,500–£3,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

marketIntelligence.h2: Mercedes R-Class Market Intelligence — What UK Specialists Told Us

signals.data: Not separately tracked in EM_Proprietary_Data; pattern-data tier
OM642 oil cooler seal leak — well-documented across all W251s

insights: Interesting insight: The R-Class is the only Mercedes model where the vehicle value (typically £1,500–£5,000 for a pre-facelift car) routinely falls below the cost of a reconditioned OM642 engine (£3,200–£5,500) — making it the Mercedes where "check the scrap value first" is the most common specialist advice. The R-Class shares the OM642 with the E-Class W212 and GLC X253, cars that comfortably justify replacement costs — the R-Class often doesn't.

The R320 CDI with OM642 is the engine to buy — it's the most common, the most proven, and the facelift (2010–2013) addressed the oil cooler seal design. The M272 petrol is thirsty and underpowered for the R-Class's 2.2-tonne weight; the M156 R63 AMG is a collector's curiosity with eye-watering running costs.

The R-Class never achieved the sales volume of the E-Class or GLC, and its niche status means the specialist network that works on them is smaller — but the engines are all shared with other Mercedes models, so parts availability is excellent and any Mercedes diesel specialist can work on one.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every R-Class Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one R-Class based on this page, make it a 2010–2013 R320 CDI facelift with the OM642 diesel and a documented oil cooler seal replacement already done. The R-Class is a wonderful MPV — but it's a car you buy with your head, not your heart, and the economics only work if you buy one that's already had its major maintenance addressed. MERCEDES R-CLASS — SECTIONS 8-14

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes R-Class Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every R-Class engine — used, reconditioned, and rebuilt supply-only — with independent labour estimates and the honest economics of when to repair vs when to scrap.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a Mercedes R-Class engine typically takes 10–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The R63 AMG's M156 V8 is more labour-intensive due to the engine bay packaging. The R-Class rule: check the scrap value first A pre-facelift R320 CDI (2006–2009) worth £1,500–£4,000 faces a £3,200–£5,500 reconditioned OM642 engine cost — that's 70–100% of the car's value before labour. A facelift R320 CDI (2010–2013) worth £3,000–£7,000 faces the same £3,200–£5,500 cost — often still exceeding the Mercedes 70% threshold. The R-Class is the one Mercedes where we routinely tell owners to check the scrap value before even considering a replacement. If the lowest viable reconditioned cost exceeds 70% of what your R-Class is worth on today's market, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. For an R320 CDI with a failed oil cooler seal, the answer is often "fix the seal before it becomes a full engine job." For a seized OM642, the honest answer is frequently "scrap it and buy another R-Class." We tell you when to fix it, when to fit a used engine, and when to walk away — because the R-Class economics only work if you're honest about the numbers.

engineEvolution.h2: How the R-Class Engine Range Evolved — 2006 to 2013

eras.keyEngines: OM642 3.0 V6 diesel • M272 3.5 V6 petrol • M273 5.5 V8 petrol • M156 6.2 AMG V8
eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes wanted to create a new segment — the "Grand Sports Tourer" — combining SUV ride height with MPV practicality and E-Class driving dynamics. The engine range reflected this ambition: V6 diesels for torque, V6/V8 petrols for performance, and the M156 for the ultra-niche R63 AMG.
eras.worthKnowing: The R63 AMG is one of the rarest Mercedes ever built — only 200 were produced. It uses the M156 6.2-litre V8 shared with the C63, E63 and SL63 — but in a 2.2-tonne MPV, it's a singularly strange experience.

eras.keyEngines: OM642 (revised) • M272 (revised) • OM651 (introduced for some markets, not UK)
eras.whyBmwChanged: The R-Class never sold in the volumes Mercedes hoped — so the facelift was a rationalisation. The V8s were dropped entirely; the range was simplified to the OM642 diesel and M272 petrol, with the OM642 receiving an improved oil cooler seal design.
eras.worthKnowing: This is the R-Class to buy: the facelift OM642 has fewer oil-cooler-seal failures, and the facelift interior and electronics are a significant step up from the pre-facelift car.

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes discontinued the R-Class globally in 2013 due to low sales — it was a niche product that never found its audience.
eras.worthKnowing: The R-Class shares its platform with the GL (now GLS) and ML (now GLE) — so parts availability remains excellent through the GLE/GLS supply chain.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a Mercedes R-Class?

profiles.verdict: The R-Class is one of the most spacious seven-seat Mercedes ever built — rear legroom is limousine-like, and the third row is genuinely usable. Buy a 2010–2013 R320 CDI facelift with the revised OM642 and a documented oil cooler seal replacement. The diesel torque (440Nm) suits the 2.2-tonne weight perfectly.

This is not a car for urban commuting — it's 5.2 metres long, 2.2 tonnes, and the diesel economy is 30–35 mpg at best. It's a motorway cruiser, not a city car. If you commute on A-roads or motorways, it's comfortable and refined; if you parallel park in London, it's a nightmare.

If you have £3,000 to spend on an R-Class, put £1,500 of it aside for immediate maintenance. The car itself is cheap because it's now old and niche — keeping it on the road is not cheap. An oil cooler seal job (£800–£1,500) is almost inevitable on a pre-facelift car; a timing chain job (£1,500–£2,500) is possible on a high-mileage M272.

The R-Class has a braked towing capacity of 2,100 kg — enough for a large caravan, horse trailer, or heavy plant trailer. The OM642's 440Nm torque makes it a competent tow vehicle, and the 2.2-tonne kerb weight provides stability. Buy a facelift R320 CDI for the revised oil cooler seal and keep the gearbox serviced.

An R-Class is not a sensible first Mercedes — it's too big, too thirsty, too niche, and too likely to present a repair bill that exceeds the car's value. A C-Class or E-Class is a better introduction to Mercedes ownership. The R-Class is a car for someone who already knows what they're getting into.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My R-Class Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Sub-headline: The R-Class's low vehicle value means the honest call is often "scrap it" — our calculator helps you make the right decision before you commit to a repair that doesn't make financial sense.

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range in this guide comes from documented failure patterns, specialist interviews, and manufacturer TSBs [PATTERN DATA], cross-referenced with 880+ OM642 and 390+ M276 UK-wide enquiries [MB-VERIFIED] where shared-engine data exists.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: If a £3,200–£5,500 reconditioned engine exceeds 70% of your R-Class's actual market value, we tell you to get a scrap valuation. That's the honest advice the R-Class market needs — and the reason this page exists.

signals.title: Vetted Mercedes
signals.text: Benz Specialists — Our network includes 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists who work on OM642 and M272 engines daily — the same specialists who know exactly how to inspect an R-Class's oil cooler seals and timing chains before you buy.

signals.title: Part of Engine Finders
signals.text: mercedesengine.uk is part of the Engine Finders family, the UK's leading engine replacement marketplace — giving you the research depth of an independent authority with a vetted route to action when you need one.
